title: Selena Commemorative Coin Act
plain_summary: The US government will make special coins to honor Selena Quintanilla, a popular Tejano singer from Texas who died in 1995. The coins will be made of gold, silver, and other metals and can only be sold in 2029. When people buy these coins, extra money from each sale will go to a museum in Corpus Christi to help them run the museum and create a special exhibit about Selena.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Selena Commemorative Coin Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Findings
sections.text: Congress states the following facts about Selena Quintanilla. Selena was born on April sixteenth nineteen seventy one. She was a music artist from South Texas who helped make Tejano music and culture popular across the United States. She was very talented and started singing when she was eight years old. Her parents loved her talent and had her perform with her family. They formed a band called Selena y Los Dinos. When she was only thirteen years old she appeared on the Johnny Canales Show. Selena first won Female Vocalist of the Year at the Tejano Music Awards in nineteen eighty six when she was fifteen. From nineteen eighty seven to nineteen ninety six she won Female Entertainer of the Year every year plus other awards like Song of the Year. The Guinness World Record recognizes Selena for having the most wins of Female Entertainer of the Year at the Tejano Music Awards. Her album Selena Live won a Grammy Award in nineteen ninety four making her the first Tejano artist to win a Grammy. Also in nineteen ninety four Texas Monthly magazine named her one of the twenty most important Texans. Her fourth album Amor Prohibido became the first Tejano record to reach number one on the Billboard Top Latin Albums chart. As of October two thousand seventeen it was the fourth best selling Latin album of all time in the United States with over one million two hundred thousand copies sold. Her final album Dreaming of You was released after she died in nineteen ninety five and was the first Tejano album to debut at the top of the United States Billboard two hundred chart. Besides her music Selena also cared about helping people. She worked with the Texas Prevention Partnership which was sponsored by the Texas Commission on Alcohol and Drug Abuse Tour to Schools. She was involved with the D.A.R.E. program and worked with the Coastal Bend Aids Foundation. Selena also made a public service announcement for the Houston Area Women's Center which is a shelter for women who have been hurt by violence in nineteen ninety three. After she died at the young age of twenty three she was honored in many ways. Then Texas Governor George W. Bush declared April sixteenth nineteen ninety five as Selena Day. A movie about Selena's life was released in nineteen ninety seven starring Jennifer Lopez in the main role. The City of Corpus Christi put up a life size bronze statue of Selena called Mirador de la Flor which means Overlook of the Flower in nineteen ninety seven to honor Selena Quintanilla Perez and her contributions to music and Corpus Christi. In two thousand eleven the United States Postal Service honored Selena as a Latin Legend by creating a memorial postage stamp. Selena did not get to achieve all of her dreams but she remains an inspiration for young Tejana and Latina women in Texas and the United States to follow their dreams.

sections.title: Section 3  Coin Specifications
sections.text: To recognize and celebrate Selena Quintanilla the Secretary of the Treasury will make and issue these coins. First they will make no more than fifty thousand five dollar gold coins. These coins will weigh eight point three five nine grams have a diameter of zero point eight five zero inches and contain at least ninety percent gold. Second they will make no more than four hundred thousand one dollar silver coins. These coins will weigh twenty six point seven three grams have a diameter of one point five zero zero inches and contain at least ninety percent silver. Third they will make no more than seven hundred fifty thousand half dollar clad coins. These coins will weigh eleven point three four grams have a diameter of one point two zero five inches and be made according to the rules for half dollar coins in section five thousand one hundred twelve part b of title thirty one United States Code. The coins made under this law will be legal tender as provided in section five thousand one hundred three of title thirty one United States Code. This means they can be used as official money. For purposes of sections five thousand one hundred thirty four and five thousand one hundred thirty six of title thirty one United States Code all coins made under this law will be considered numismatic items. This means they are special collectible coins rather than regular money for everyday use.

sections.title: Section 4  Designs of Coins
sections.text: The designs for the coins made under this law must represent Selena Quintanilla and her life and achievements. At least one front design must show an image of Selena Quintanilla. On each coin made under this law there must be a marking that shows the value of the coin an inscription of the year two thousand twenty nine and inscriptions of the words Liberty In God We Trust United States of America and E Pluribus Unum. The designs for the coins made under this law will be chosen by the Secretary after talking with the Friends of the Corpus Christi Museum of Science and History. The Citizens Coinage Advisory Committee will review the designs.

sections.title: Section 5  Issuance of Coins
sections.text: Coins made under this law will be issued in uncirculated and proof qualities. Uncirculated means the coins have never been used as money. Proof means the coins are made with special care to look perfect. The Secretary may issue coins made under this law only during the calendar year that begins on January first two thousand twenty nine.

sections.title: Section 6  Sale of Coins
sections.text: The coins issued under this law will be sold by the Secretary at a price based on the sum of the face value of the coins the surcharge provided in section seven part a with respect to such coins and the cost of designing and issuing the coins including labor materials dies use of machinery overhead expenses marketing and shipping. The Secretary will make bulk sales of the coins issued under this law at a reasonable discount. This means people who buy many coins at once will pay less per coin. The Secretary will accept prepaid orders for the coins made under this law before the coins are issued. Sale prices for prepaid orders will be at a reasonable discount.

sections.title: Section 7  Surcharges
sections.text: All sales of coins made under this law will include a surcharge as follows. A surcharge of thirty five dollars per coin for the five dollar coin. A surcharge of ten dollars per coin for the one dollar coin. A surcharge of five dollars per coin for the half dollar coin. Subject to section five thousand one hundred thirty four part f of title thirty one United States Code all surcharges received by the Secretary from the sale of coins issued under this law will be paid to the Friends of the Corpus Christi Museum of Science and History. The money will be used for general expenses associated with carrying out the mission of the Friends of the Corpus Christi Museum of Science and History including costs for museum operations and a Selena exhibit. The Friends of the Corpus Christi Museum of Science and History must follow the audit requirements of section five thousand one hundred thirty four part f part two of title thirty one United States Code regarding the amounts received under this section. However no surcharge may be included with respect to issuing any coin under this law during a calendar year if at the time of such issuing the coin would cause the number of commemorative coin programs issued during such year to exceed the annual limit of two commemorative coin programs under section five thousand one hundred twelve part m part one of title thirty one United States Code. The Secretary may issue guidance to carry out this rule.

sections.title: Section 8  Financial Assurances
sections.text: The Secretary must take actions necessary to ensure that making and issuing coins under this law will not result in any net cost to the Federal Government. The Secretary must also ensure that no funds including applicable surcharges are given to any recipient designated in section seven part b until the total cost of designing and issuing all of the coins authorized by this law including labor materials dies use of machinery overhead expenses marketing and shipping is recovered by the United States Treasury. This must be consistent with sections five thousand one hundred twelve part m and five thousand one hundred thirty four part f of title thirty one United States Code.
